beginner.situation: You brought your queen out early to attack a pawn while your kingside pieces were still undeveloped.
beginner.coaching: That attack is tempting, but it lets your opponent gain time by chasing your queen while your king stays in the center. A better move is Nf3: develop a piece, help control the center, and get ready to castle. Takeaway: before using the queen for attacks, develop a piece and make your king safe.

beginner.situation: You made a pawn move while your knight was attacked more times than it was defended.
beginner.coaching: The pawn move does not answer the real danger, so the knight can simply be taken. Play Be2 instead: it adds a defender and also helps you get closer to castling. Takeaway: when a piece is attacked, first count attackers and defenders before starting a new idea.

beginner.situation: You gave a check with your queen instead of castling while your king was still in the center.
beginner.coaching: The check looks active, but if it is blocked, your queen may just have to move again. Castling is stronger here because it makes your king safe and brings a rook closer to the center. Takeaway: a safe king is usually worth more than a quick check.

intermediate.situation: You grabbed a wing pawn with your queen while your rooks and minor pieces were not fully active.
intermediate.coaching: Winning a pawn is nice, but this lets your opponent attack the queen with tempo and take over the center. A better move is O-O: finish king safety first, then look for Re1 and pressure on the open or half-open file. Takeaway: when you are behind in development, choose activity and safety before pawn-hunting.

intermediate.situation: You traded your active bishop for a knight that was not causing a serious problem.
intermediate.coaching: That trade leaves you with the more passive bishop and gives away a piece that was helping control important central squares. A better move is Be3: keep the active bishop, connect your pieces, and prepare to put a rook on an open file. Takeaway: do not trade a good piece just because you can; ask which piece will matter more in the next plan.

intermediate.situation: You pushed a pawn to attack a knight, but it weakened squares around your king and opened lines for your opponent.
intermediate.coaching: The pawn push gains a tempo, but the new weaknesses may last longer than the attack on the knight. A steadier move is Re1: improve the rook, support the center, and be ready to meet a capture by recapturing with a piece. Takeaway: before chasing a piece with a pawn, check what squares and lines you are leaving behind.

advanced.situation: You released the central tension too early, giving your opponent an easy recapture and a clear square for a piece.
advanced.coaching: The exchange solves your opponent’s coordination problem and removes the pressure they had to manage. A stronger practical move is Re1: keep the tension, support a central break, and make it harder for them to free their position comfortably. Takeaway: when the center is tense, do not clarify it unless the resulting position favors your pieces.

advanced.situation: You won a pawn on the queenside while your king was still exposed and your back rank pieces were undeveloped.
advanced.coaching: The pawn grab is understandable, but it gives your opponent quick tempi against the queen and time to open the center. Castling is the more instructive move: secure the king first, then use Qe2 or Rd1 to finish development and return to the extra target later. Takeaway: if your king is not safe, delay material gains that give the opponent free activity.

advanced.situation: You attacked on the kingside without first stopping your opponent’s central break.
advanced.coaching: The attack looks natural, but their central push can hit your pieces and make your own king less secure. A better move is c3: it supports the center, limits the break, and prepares to improve your worst piece before committing to the attack. Takeaway: strong attacks often begin by taking away the defender’s best counterplay.
